Short answer
- Snipara’s core value proposition is Context Optimization as a Service: it massively reduces and compacts large documentation into a small, highly relevant context (≈90% reduction — e.g., 500K → ~5K tokens) that clients pass into their own LLMs.

Key supporting points (from the docs)
- 90% context reduction: compresses large doc sets into ~5K relevant tokens.
- Token budgeting: honors a client-specified max_tokens budget when returning context.
- LLM-agnostic / no vendor lock-in: clients use their own LLM (Snipara does not run inference).
- Lower cost / high margins: Snipara charges for context optimization (compute only), not LLM inference.
- Features that enable this: hybrid keyword+semantic search, smart chunking, relevance scoring, session context persistence, and metadata-rich responses (file paths, line numbers, scores).

These points are described throughout the product overview, product philosophy, and solution sections of the documentation.

Summary — how Snipara’s token budgeting works (facts from the docs)

Core idea
- Snipara selects and returns an optimized set of documentation sections that fit inside an output token budget instead of returning full documents. It measures tokens with tiktoken and enforces a hard token budget when building the context sent to an LLM.

Key components and behavior
- Primary tool: rlm_context_query — the context optimization tool that accepts:
  - query: string
  - max_tokens: int (default shown as 4000)
  - search_mode: "keyword" | "semantic" | "hybrid"
  - include_metadata: bool
  - (implementations also show optional flags like include_shared and budget_allocation)
- Token counting: uses tiktoken (cl100k_base encoder). pyproject should include tiktoken>=0.5.0.
- Candidate ranking: sections are scored (relevance_score). The engine ranks by relevance.
- Greedy selection: sections are included in descending relevance order until the token budget would be exceeded.
- Partial sections: if a full section does not fit but some tokens remain, the engine uses smart_truncate() to truncate the section to the remaining tokens, cutting at sentence boundaries ('.', '!', '?'), appending "..." and marking that section as truncated.
- Metadata returned per section: title, content, file path, line ranges, relevance_score, token_count (and a truncated flag when applicable).
- Response includes: selected sections, total_tokens used, truncated flag (whether the overall result hit the budget), session_context (if any), and suggestions.

Budget allocation strategies (how budget may be split)
- Documentation shows category-based allocations and priority rules that can be applied when merging shared context collections:
  - One example allocation (Token Budget Algorithm):
    - CATEGORY_BUDGETS: MANDATORY 0.4, BEST_PRACTICES 0.3, GUIDELINES 0.2, REFERENCE 0.1
    - CATEGORY_PRIORITY: MANDATORY highest, then BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE
  - Another example (TypeScript DEFAULT_ALLOCATION used in budget-allocation code):
    - project: 0.40 (project docs prioritized)
    - MANDATORY: 0.25
    - BEST_PRACTICES: 0.20
    - GUIDELINES: 0.10
    - REFERENCE: 0.05
- Allocation process (documented example):
  - Project docs are allocated a project budget first.
  - Shared docs are grouped by category (MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE) and each category is allocated a slice of the total budget.
  - Within a category, docs are sorted by relevance and selected until the category budget is used up (allocateWithinBudget).
- The system supports custom budget_allocation overrides when calling the context tool.

Other operational notes in the docs
- Session context: docs mention session context can be included (docs note “session context inclusion (up to 20% of budget)”).
- System instructions are auto-injected into the process.
- The rlm_context_query implementation and examples return usage/diagnostic info (search_time_ms, sections_scanned, sources counts).
- There are helper flows for merged shared context (get_merged_context) and for multi-query / decomposed query handling that respect per-query budgets.

What you’ll see in an rlm_context_query response (example fields)
- sections: list of { title, content, file, lines, relevance_score, token_count, (truncated?) }
- total_tokens: sum tokens included
- truncated: overall flag if budget was filled
- session_context: previous context (if present)
- suggestions: additional docs to check

- Token-budget input
  - Context queries accept a max_tokens budget (default 4000) that limits how many tokens of context the tool will return.

- Accurate token counting

- Candidate selection (greedy, relevance-first)
  - Candidate document/section matches are ranked by relevance.
  - Each candidate’s token cost is measured.
  - Sections are selected greedily in order of relevance until the budget would be exceeded.
  - If a section would partially fit, a smart truncation is applied to fill the remaining tokens (see below).

- Session & system context

  - System instructions are auto-injected into the process.

- Returned metadata and usage info

Based on the provided documentation, Snipara’s token-budgeting works as follows:

High-level idea
- The context optimization tool (rlm_context_query) returns the most relevant document sections that fit a requested token budget (max_tokens). It computes token counts and selects content to maximize relevance while staying within that budget.

Key components and behavior
- Request parameters
  - query: the user question.
  - max_tokens (default 4000): the token budget for the returned context.
  - search_mode: "keyword" | "semantic" | "hybrid".
  - include_metadata: whether to include file/line information.
  - options like include_shared / budget_allocation are supported in merged-context variants.

- Token counting
  - Uses tiktoken with the cl100k_base encoder (GPT‑4/Claude compatible).
  - Section token counts are computed with encoder.encode(section.content) length.

- Selection algorithm
  - Candidate sections are searched (keyword, semantic, or fused/hybrid).
  - Candidates are ranked by relevance.
  - Greedy selection: iterate highest-relevance first and add whole sections while total_tokens + section_tokens <= max_tokens.
  - If a section would overflow but some tokens remain, a partial section is included via smart_truncate to fit the remaining tokens, then selection stops.

- Smart truncation
  - Truncates content to fit remaining tokens.
  - Truncation occurs at sentence boundaries (split on . ! ?), avoiding mid-sentence cuts.
  - Adds an ellipsis ("...") and marks the section as truncated in metadata.

- Metadata and response
  - Returned section metadata includes title, content, file path, line ranges, relevance_score, token_count, and truncated flag when applicable.
  - Response includes sections[], total_tokens, truncated (true if budget fully used), session_context (if any), and suggestions (e.g., related files).
  - The tool also returns operational metrics (search time, sections scanned).

Budget allocation and categories (shared context / merged context)
- Default category allocations (used for shared/merged contexts):
  - MANDATORY: 40%
  - BEST_PRACTICES: 30%
  - GUIDELINES: 20%
  - REFERENCE: 10%
- Category priority determines processing order (MANDATORY highest, REFERENCE lowest).
- Project docs are allocated first (default project allocation ~40%), then shared documents are allocated by category.
- Allocation routine:
  - Group docs by source/category.
  - Compute per-category/per-source token budgets (defaults can be overridden).
  - Within each budget, sort docs by relevance and include docs until the category budget is exhausted; remaining docs are marked unallocated.
- Hard limits and safeguards exist (examples: MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION, MAX_DOCS_PER_COLLECTION, and a minimum relevance threshold).

Other notes from the docs
- Session context can be injected (implementation notes mention allowing up to ~20% of the budget for session context).
- Tools expose both project-only and merged (project + shared collections) modes.
- The implementation generates “suggestions” (related docs) alongside the selected sections.

Summary — what shared context collections are
- Shared context collections are team-managed collections of reusable instructions (coding standards, best practices, guidelines, reference material) that are automatically available to all projects linked to the collection.
- Purpose: enforce consistent team rules (e.g., security and architecture mandates), surface recommended patterns, and provide reusable templates and reference content for every developer and every query.

How they work (workflow)
1. Admin creates a Shared Context Collection (team-level).
2. Admin uploads documents to the collection (e.g., with rlm_upload_shared_document).
3. Collections are linked to projects via the dashboard (Project → Shared Contexts → Link Collection).
4. When you run queries (rlm_shared_context or rlm_context_query), the linked shared collections are merged and injected into the query context so project work automatically follows team rules.

Key behaviors and APIs shown in the docs
- rlm_list_collections(include_public=true): list available collections (team private + public).
- rlm_upload_shared_document(collection_id, title, content, category, priority): add documents to a collection.
- rlm_shared_context(categories=[...], max_tokens=... , include_project_docs=...): returns merged shared context (documents categorized and token-budgeted).
- rlm_context_query(query, include_shared_context=true, shared_context_budget_percent=...): when used, shared context is loaded first and then project docs are queried with the remaining budget.
- rlm_list_templates / rlm_get_template: list and fetch prompt templates stored in collections.

Document categories and priority (how content is prioritized)
- Categories (priority order and typical token-budget allocation):
  - MANDATORY — Highest priority (non‑negotiable rules: security, architecture). Example allocation: 40% of shared-context budget.
  - BEST_PRACTICES — High priority (recommended patterns). Example allocation: 30%.
  - GUIDELINES — Medium (helpful suggestions). Example allocation: 20%.
  - REFERENCE — Low (background info, examples). Example allocation: 10%.
- Collections support per-document priority (0–100) to influence ordering within categories.

Query behavior and token budgeting
- Shared context is included before project documents when answering a query (shared context is loaded first).
- Token budget is split between shared context and project docs (examples show defaults like 30% for shared context, with the documents within shared context further allocated across categories per the percentages above). The rlm_shared_context response can include a budget_allocation breakdown.

Other notes from the docs
- Shared context makes it possible to auto-enforce team rules across all project queries and to inject standards at session start (examples show using rlm_shared_context then rlm_inject to apply standards to subsequent queries).
- Team plan (TEAM or ENTERPRISE) is required for team/shared-collections features — not available on Free/Pro plans.
- Admin or owner permissions are required to create team contexts/collections (APIs enforce admin-only creation).

What they contain

- Items can include coding standards, security requirements, architecture patterns, and prompt templates.

How they work (workflow)
1. Team admin creates a Shared Context Collection and uploads documents (e.g., via rlm_upload_shared_document or the dashboard).
2. The collection is linked to one or more projects (Project > Shared Contexts → Link Collection).
3. When a project runs rlm_context_query (or rlm_shared_context), the shared collection(s) are merged into the query context and injected automatically so team rules are available to the agent/AI.
4. Collections are returned and prioritized by category and per-document priority (0–100).

Token-budget & prioritization (how content is prioritized in queries)
- Shared context documents are categorized and prioritized. Category allocation inside the shared context is:
  - MANDATORY — highest priority (40% of shared-context budget)
  - BEST_PRACTICES — (30%)
  - GUIDELINES — (20%)
  - REFERENCE — (10%)
- Shared context is included before project docs and is allocated a configurable portion of the query token budget (examples in the docs show a default shared_context_budget_percent = 30%, but other examples use 40%); project docs receive the remaining budget. Short answer
- Snipara implements the OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant (RFC 8628). A CLI (or other device) requests a device/user code, the user visits a verification URL and enters the code in a browser, and the CLI polls for the access token until the user authorizes.

Step-by-step (facts from the docs)
1. Start device authorization
   - Endpoint: POST /api/oauth/device/code
   - Typical request body: { "client_id": "snipara_cli" } (an optional auto_provision boolean may be provided)
   - Response includes:
     - device_code (opaque string the client will poll with)
     - user_code (human-readable code, e.g. "ABCD-1234")
     - verification_uri (e.g. https://snipara.com/device)
     - verification_uri_complete (a URL including the code)
     - expires_in (e.g. 900 seconds)
     - interval (polling interval, e.g. 5 seconds)

2. User authorizes in browser
   - User visits verification_uri (or verification_uri_complete) and enters the user_code (or follows the complete URL).
   - Server shows a device authorization page (GET /api/oauth/device/authorize accepts a code query param).

3. Client polls for token
   - Endpoint: POST /api/oauth/device/token
   - Poll request body:
     {
       "grant_type": "urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:device_code",
       "device_code": "<device_code>",
       "client_id": "snipara_cli"
     }
   - While the user has not yet approved, response can be { "error": "authorization_pending" }.
   - On success the response contains:
     - access_token (e.g. "snipara_at_...")
     - token_type: "Bearer"
     - expires_in (example: 2592000)
     - refresh_token (e.g. "snipara_rt_...")

Other relevant facts from the docs
- This flow is used so users can authenticate without copying API keys (described as “secure MCP authentication without copying API keys”).
- Authorization header format for OAuth tokens: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
- The MCP server prefers stored OAuth tokens in ~/.snipara/tokens.json first, and falls back to API key authentication (X-API-Key: rlm_...) if no valid OAuth token is found.
- There are related endpoints for token refresh (/api/oauth/token) and revoke (/api/oauth/revoke), and token management UI endpoints (/api/settings/tokens).
- If you want to force API-key usage instead of OAuth, the client can be configured to ignore OAuth (SNIPARA_IGNORE_OAUTH environment variable) or you can remove ~/.snipara/tokens.json.

Snipara uses a strict layered architecture (separation of concerns) with a top-to-bottom import hierarchy and clear responsibilities:

- Layers (top → bottom / higher → lower):
  - Routes: app/api/ — thin HTTP route handlers (authenticate, validate with Zod, delegate to service/query, return response).
  - Jobs / Orchestrators: lib/jobs/ — processors/orchestrators (can be used by routes).
  - Services: lib/services/ — external API adapters (uses lazy initialization + retry for clients).
  - Repositories: lib/db/queries/ — centralized CRUD / repository layer (ALL DB access goes here; route handlers must not call Prisma directly).
  - Types & Utils: types/, lib/utils/ — shared, pure code with NO dependencies on other layers.

- Key rules enforced by the pattern:
  - Lower layers cannot import from higher layers.
  - types/ and utils/ must have no dependencies on other layers.
  - All database queries must go through lib/db/queries/ (repository pattern).
  - Route handlers must remain thin and delegate to services/queries.

- Related patterns used within the layers:
  - Repository pattern for DB access (permission checks kept in repository functions).
  - Services pattern for external integrations (lazy client initialization and retry logic).
  - Route handler pattern (authenticate → validate → delegate → respond).

These points are taken directly from the project documentation.

- If the question is multi-part, large-scope, or likely needs ordered/parallel investigation across many docs: rlm_decompose (then execute the sub-queries with rlm_multi_query and synthesize).

Short answer
- Start with rlm_context_query for normal documentation questions — it’s the primary, full‑featured semantic search tool for “How does X work?” style queries.
- Use rlm_decompose when the question is complex or large (multi-part, covers many documents) and needs to be split into sub-queries; pair it with rlm_multi_query to run those sub-queries and synthesize a final answer.

Why (documentation-backed)
- rlm_context_query: described as the primary full‑featured documentation query (semantic search, ranked results). The MCP workflow explicitly says to call rlm_context_query first for questions like “How does authentication work?”.
- rlm_decompose: intended to “break complex questions into sub-queries” (the docs show rlm_decompose → rlm_multi_query as the pattern for complex questions and include an implementation example that builds sub-queries, dependencies, and a suggested sequence).
- Recursive/near‑infinite context: the docs show rlm_decompose + rlm_multi_query (and related recursive tools) are how the system handles very large or multi‑step queries that exceed a single context window.

Practical decision rule (from docs)
1. Ask a regular doc question → rlm_context_query (default).
2. If the answer is insufficient, or your question is explicitly complex/multi‑part or covers many documents → run rlm_decompose to get sub‑queries and an execution plan, then use rlm_multi_query to execute them.

Related tips from docs
- For quick/simple queries you can use rlm_ask.
- For pattern/regex searches use rlm_search.

Summary of authentication options (from the docs) and how they differ in security

1) Project API Key
- What: A project-scoped API key with prefix rlm_.
- How used: Sent as X-API-Key: rlm_... header (used by MCP and Inject endpoints).
- Implementation/security notes (documented):
  - Stored hashed with SHA‑256 in the DB (auth.py:29-95).
  - Server computes a hash and compares to the stored keyHash (validate_api_key()).
  - Only the first 8 chars are shown in UI/logs (masked display).
  - Project keys only grant access to a single project.
- Lifecycle/management: Can be created/revoked via dashboard/APIs (documentation references to API key create/revoke).
- Security implications (as documented): long‑lived keys looked up by hash; scope limited to a project (more granular).

2) Team API Key
- What: A team-scoped API key with the same rlm_ format.
- How used: Sent as X-API-Key: rlm_... header (works across all projects in the team).
- Implementation/security notes:
  - Same hashing/lookup approach as project keys; system falls back to team key if no project key is found.
  - Intended for wider access (one key for all projects in a team).
- Security implications (as documented): broader access surface than a project key (single key can access multiple projects); still stored hashed in DB.

3) OAuth access token (Device Flow / MCP authentication)
- What: OAuth 2.0 device flow tokens (access tokens prefixed snipara_at_ and refresh tokens snipara_rt_).
- How used: Sent as Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_... header (MCP endpoint supports this).
- Implementation/security notes (documented):
  - Token format: snipara_at_ prefix.
  - Validation: DB lookup + expiry check (validate_oauth_token()) and scope enforcement (read/write scope enforcement).
  - Device flow conforms to RFC 8628: CLI shows code + URL for browser authentication (no copy/paste of API keys).
  - Tokens can be refreshed via /api/oauth/token using refresh_token.
  - Tokens can be listed (GET /api/settings/tokens) and revoked (DELETE /api/settings/tokens/[tokenId]).
- Security implications (as documented): tokens are time‑limited and revocable, scopes enforced, and the device flow reduces need to copy/store long-lived API keys.

Other relevant operational details from the docs
- Endpoint differences:
  - MCP server (api.snipara.com/mcp/{slug}) accepts either X-API-Key or Authorization: Bearer.
  - Inject API (www.snipara.com/api/projects/{slug}/automation/inject) accepts X-API-Key only.
- Client priority behavior:
  - The client checks for a stored OAuth token at ~/.snipara/tokens.json first and falls back to API key from environment variables if no valid OAuth token is available.
  - The docs show removing ~/.snipara/tokens.json to “force” API key authentication.
- Failure behavior:
  - Expired/invalid OAuth tokens produce a 401 with messaging to re-authenticate; tokens can be refreshed or revoked per the documented endpoints.

Plain comparison (documented differences)
- API keys: rlm_ prefix, SHA‑256 hashed in DB, persistent until revoked, can be project-scoped (narrower) or team-scoped (broader). Sent via X-API-Key.
- OAuth tokens: snipara_at_ prefix, validated via DB + expiry + scopes, time-limited and refreshable, revocable, obtained via device flow (no key copying). Sent via Authorization: Bearer.

Summary of authentication options (from the docs) and how they differ in security

1) Project API Key (X-API-Key: rlm_...)
- What it is: API key with prefix "rlm_".
- Where used: Sent in the X-API-Key header; supported by MCP endpoints and the Inject API. Example:
  - MCP: X-API-Key: rlm_...
  - Inject API: X-API-Key: rlm_...
- Implementation details from docs:
  - Keys are SHA-256 hashed in the database (validate_api_key() hashes incoming key and compares).
  - Lookup tries project-specific keys first, then falls back to team keys.
  - UI/logs only show the first 8 characters of keys.
- Security characteristics (documented facts):
  - Static token presented in requests and validated by hash lookup. (No documented automatic expiry for API keys in the provided docs.)
  - Project keys grant access to a single project; therefore their scope is narrower than team keys.

2) Team API Key (X-API-Key: rlm_...)
- What it is: Same format as project API keys, but associated with a team.
- Where used: Sent in X-API-Key header; used when you need access across a team’s projects.
- Security characteristics (documented facts):
  - Broader access than project API keys (access all projects in a team). Because of broader scope, team keys have wider privileges than project keys.

3) OAuth access token (Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...)
- What it is: OAuth tokens prefixed with "snipara_at_".
- Where used: MCP endpoints accept Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_... (MCP supports both OAuth and API keys).
- Device flow: The docs describe a standard OAuth 2.0 Device Flow (RFC 8628) for CLI/browser authentication (user runs CLI, visits URL, enters code).
- Token lifecycle/management (documented facts):
  - Access tokens include expires_in and a refresh_token can be issued; token refresh endpoint exists (/api/oauth/token with grant_type=refresh_token).
  - Validation is a DB lookup + expiry check (validate_oauth_token()).
  - There are endpoints to list and revoke tokens: GET /api/settings/tokens and DELETE /api/settings/tokens/[tokenId].
  - If an OAuth token is invalid or expired the documented 401 message is returned and user must re-authenticate.
- Security characteristics (documented facts):
  - OAuth tokens are time-limited (expires_in present) and support refresh tokens and revocation.
  - OAuth supports scopes (read/write enforcement is mentioned in implementation notes), so finer-grained permissioning is available.

Operational behavior and differences (documented)
- Priority: the MCP client/server checks for a stored OAuth token at ~/.snipara/tokens.json first, and falls back to an API key from environment variables if no valid OAuth token is present. (Docs also show how to remove ~/.snipara/tokens.json to force API key usage.)
- Endpoint differences: the Inject API examples only show X-API-Key (rlm_) usage; MCP supports both Authorization: Bearer (OAuth) and X-API-Key.
- Management: OAuth tokens can be listed and revoked via API endpoints (allowing explicit revocation); API key display/logging is limited (first 8 chars shown) and keys are stored hashed in DB.

Practical security implications (based on documented facts)
- OAuth tokens provide time-limited credentials, refresh tokens, scopes, and explicit revocation endpoints — these features enable short-lived, scope-limited, and revocable access.
- API keys are static credentials validated via a hash lookup and come in project-scoped and team-scoped variants; team keys are broader in access than project keys.
- Which to use depends on your use case: the docs explicitly present the device flow as a way to avoid copying API keys for MCP authentication.

Summary of authentication options (facts confirmed by the docs) and how they differ in security:

1) API Key (project key and team key)
- How to use:
  - Send header: X-API-Key: rlm_...
  - Server hashes the key with SHA-256 and looks up the hash in the ApiKey table (project keys checked first, then team keys).
  - Keys support lookup by project ID or slug.
- Variants:
  - Project API key — scoped to a single project.
  - Team API key — works across all projects in the team (broader scope).
- Security-related facts:
  - Keys have the rlm_ prefix and are SHA-256 hashed in the database (server stores only the hash).
  - Only the first 8 characters are shown in UI/logs (masking to reduce leakage).
  - Team keys are more convenient for CI/CD but have broader access (team-wide).
- Endpoints:
  - Inject API (REST) accepts X-API-Key only.
  - MCP endpoint accepts X-API-Key as well.
- Management:
  - Team API keys can be created/listed/revoked via team API-key endpoints.

2) OAuth tokens (Device Flow)
- How to use:
  - Device Flow (RFC 8628): CLI issues a device code, user authenticates in browser; client polls /api/oauth/device/token until access is granted.
  - Authorization header: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
  - Tokens have a snipara_at_ prefix; refresh tokens use snipara_rt_ prefix.
- Security-related facts:
  - OAuth tokens are validated with a DB lookup and expiry check; scopes (read/write) are enforced.
  - Tokens have expiration and a refresh flow (POST /api/oauth/token with grant_type=refresh_token returns a new access_token and refresh_token).
  - Device flow avoids copying API keys into client flows (reduces some risks of key leakage).
- Client behavior and storage:
  - The client stores OAuth tokens in ~/.snipara/tokens.json and will use stored OAuth tokens before falling back to API keys from environment variables.
  - You can remove or move ~/.snipara/tokens.json to force API key authentication.
- Management:
  - There are endpoints to list tokens (GET /api/settings/tokens) and revoke tokens (DELETE /api/settings/tokens/[tokenId]).
- Failure mode:
  - An expired or revoked OAuth access token returns a 401 with a message prompting re-authentication.

3) Priority and endpoint differences (important for security choices)
- The MCP client prefers a stored OAuth token (tokens.json) first, then falls back to API key from environment variables.
- MCP server accepts either Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_... (OAuth) or X-API-Key: rlm_... (API key).
- Inject API uses X-API-Key only.

Security comparison (concise)
- OAuth (device flow):
  - Stronger for short-lived, scoped access: tokens expire, scopes are enforced, refresh flow exists, and tokens can be revoked from the dashboard / API.
  - Device flow avoids handing long-lived secrets to the CLI, reducing key-leak risk.
- API keys:
  - Simpler and convenient (especially team keys for CI), but are broader in scope (team keys) and represent a secret that must be protected by the client and environment.
  - Server-side storage is protected by hashing (SHA-256) and UI/log masking reduces accidental exposure.
- Operational note:
  - Removing the client token file forces use of API keys (documented command to rm or mv ~/.snipara/tokens.json).

Details (documented behavior)

1. Query order
- rlm_context_query loads shared best-practices first, then queries local project scope with the remaining token budget (section: "MCP Query Order").

2. rlm_context_query parameters
- rlm_context_query/rlm_context_query-like calls accept an include_shared_context flag and a shared_context_budget_percent parameter (example JSON shows "shared_context_budget_percent": 30) so you can control how much of the total budget is reserved for shared context.

3. Two documented allocation examples (note: both are present in the docs)
- rlm_shared_context example (merged shared-context response) shows a shared-only category allocation of:
  - MANDATORY: 40%
  - BEST_PRACTICES: 30%
  - GUIDELINES: 20%
  - REFERENCE: 10%
  (this is the budget breakdown inside shared context shown in the example response.)
- The allocateTokenBudget implementation (apps/web/lib/services/token-budget.ts) defines a DEFAULT_ALLOCATION used by the allocation routine:
  - project: 0.40 (40% to project docs)
  - MANDATORY: 0.25
  - BEST_PRACTICES: 0.20
  - GUIDELINES: 0.10
  - REFERENCE: 0.05
  and the function accepts a customAllocation to override these values.

4. How allocateTokenBudget actually applies the budget (implementation facts)
- It groups docs into project docs and shared docs by category.
- It allocates the project budget first: projectBudget = floor(totalBudget * allocation.project) and selects project docs to fit that budget.
- Then it allocates each shared category: categoryBudget = floor(totalBudget * allocation[category]) and selects docs for each category.
- Within each allocation, docs are sorted by relevanceScore (if present) and included until tokensUsed + doc.tokenCount would exceed the category budget. Docs that don't fit are marked allocated: false and allocationReason: 'exceeded budget'.
- The function supports passing customAllocation to change the default splits.

5. Hard limits
- There are additional limits enforced: MAX_COLLECTIONS_PER_PROJECT, MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION (100,000), MAX_DOCS_PER_COLLECTION, and a MIN_RELEVANCE_THRESHOLD. enforceCollectionLimits will truncate a collection to fit MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION.

Summary / practical effect
- Shared context is included first and receives its own reserved token allocation (configurable via parameters). The system then uses the remaining budget for project docs.
- Category percentages can come from the shared-context merging logic (examples show 40/30/20/10 for shared categories) or from the allocation routine’s DEFAULT_ALLOCATION (which includes a project share and uses 40/25/20/10/5 split). You can override behavior via rlm_context_query parameters (shared_context_budget_percent) or allocateTokenBudget’s customAllocation.
